summary: Dr. Meir Stampfer discusses his transition from clinical medicine to epidemiology, driven by a desire to focus on preventive medicine. He highlights his work with major longitudinal cohorts to advance cancer prevention and praises the collaborative, methodologically rigorous environment at Harvard.

principles.statement: Epidemiology should focus on expanding practical methods for disease prevention.
principles.rationale: He shifted from clinical medicine to epidemiology, and specifically into cancer epidemiology, because there was a significant lack of prevention work being done in the field.
principles.source_quote: my goal has always been to expand the range of practical methods and approaches that we can take for prevention

principles.statement: A nurturing academic community with shared values is crucial for advancing public health.
principles.rationale: Despite the presence of brilliant and potentially intimidating minds, a welcoming and collaborative environment fosters better progress toward the shared goal of improving public health.
principles.source_quote: it really is a sense of community and a commonality of values to improve public health so that that really makes it a special place

signature_quotes.text: my goal has always been to expand the range of practical methods and approaches that we can take for prevention
signature_quotes.context: Discussing his overarching career objectives in epidemiology.

signature_quotes.text: very little prevention work had been done in cancer epidemiology so that was the big draw for me as a means to expand that field
signature_quotes.context: Explaining his shift toward cancer research after starting in cardiovascular epidemiology.

signature_quotes.text: it really is a sense of community and a commonality of values to improve public health so that that really makes it a special place
signature_quotes.context: Describing the culture at Harvard's School of Public Health.
